payload.text: I want to build a medical Examination library with in depth descriptions of all Physical examinations that doctors perform. I want it to be structured similar to the Talley and O'Conner textbook in terms of taxonomy and categorisation. Further inspiration can be taken from geeky medics or OSCEstop

**Defining the Scope**

I'm focused on defining the project scope, envisioning a "Medical Examination Library" much like Talley and O'Connor, Geeky Medics, and OSCEstop. I'm leaning towards a React SPA with a clean, medical UI/UX using Tailwind CSS. The Gemini API will power content generation and deeper dives into the information. I'm currently envisioning its search functionalities as a cornerstone.


**Outlining Core Features**

I've been mapping out the core features. I'm thinking of step-by-step guides for each physical exam category (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, Abdominal, Neurological, Musculoskeletal) with "Deep Dive" functionalities, powered by Gemini, for clinical correlation. I'm also considering OSCE checklists and focusing on professional visual design for high contrast and readability. The frontend is planned with React 18, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS, icons from Lucide React, Gemini API, and React hooks.


**Detailing Project Implementation**

I'm now diving into the implementation details. I'm structuring the project with specific directories: `components`, `services`, and `constants` for clarity. I'm focusing on key files like `ExamDetail.tsx` and `gemini.ts` for step-by-step guides and AI integration. The prompt structure is defined: upon clinical sign clicks, I will trigger Gemini with specific system-context prompts to expand on the information. I'm keeping the design clean with white backgrounds, blue accents, and rounded corners.

payload.text:
Implement a powerful search and filtering system for the medical examination library. Users should be able to search by examination name, body system, condition, or keywords. The filters should allow users to narrow down results by category, specialty, or examination type, enabling quick access to relevant information.

For each physical examination described in the medical examination library, include relevant high-quality images and embedded videos that visually demonstrate the correct technique. Ensure the multimedia content is clearly labeled and integrated contextually with the textual descriptions, similar to the visual aids found on Geeky Medics or OSCEstop.

For each physical examination in the medical library, develop an interactive checklist. The checklist should break down the examination into sequential, actionable steps. Users should be able to mark steps as completed, and the system should provide feedback or highlight the next step, mimicking a practical OSCE-style guide.

**Developing Core Features**

I'm currently focused on the core features for the MedEx Library. I'm prioritizing the implementation of a robust search and filtering system, allowing users to efficiently search by name, system, condition, and keywords. I'm also considering how best to integrate high-quality images and embedded videos to enhance the user experience.


**Implementing Interactive Checklist**

I'm now integrating an interactive checklist into each exam view, like a digital OSCE guide, to improve the learning process. I've updated the `types.ts` file to include `videoUrl` and `imageUrl` properties for `ExamStep` and `Examination`, and also updating `constants.ts` with placeholder multimedia URLs, and I'm updating `App.tsx` with a new search filter state to support enhanced search functionality for systems. I'm focusing on getting that checklist working in the `ExamView.tsx` component.

entries.description: Updated types to include multimedia properties and checklist support.
Enhanced EXAMINATIONS data with video IDs, keywords, and realistic medical imagery placeholders.

Position at 45 degrees.', imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1576091160550-2173dba999ef?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=400' },
      { id: '2', category: 'Inspection', title: 'General Inspection', description: 'Observe for shortness of breath, malar flush, cyanosis, and syndromic features.' },

      { id: '4', category: 'Palpation', title: 'Radial Pulse', description: 'Assess rate, rhythm, and character. Check for radio-radial delay.' },

      { id: '6', category: 'Inspection', title: 'Face & Neck', description: 'Check eyes for xanthelasma and conjunctival pallor. Check mouth for central cyanosis.' },

      { id: '8', category: 'Auscultation', title: 'Heart Sounds', description: 'Listen to all 4 valve areas with diaphragm and bell. Perform dynamic maneuvers.', imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1505751172876-fa1923c5c528?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=400' },
      { id: '9', category: 'Conclusion', title: 'Peripheral Oedema', description: 'Check for pitting oedema in the sacrum and lower limbs.' }
    ]
  },

      { id: 'r3', category: 'Inspection', title: 'Hands', description: 'Look for clubbing, tar staining, and CO2 retention flap (asterixis).', imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1559757175-5700dde675bc?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=400' },
      { id: 'r4', category: 'Palpation', title: 'Trachea', description: 'Check for tracheal deviation and cricosternal distance.' },
      { id: 'r5', category: 'Palpation', title: 'Expansion', description: 'Assess chest expansion symmetrically in both upper and lower zones.' },
      { id: 'r6', category: 'Percussion', title: 'Percussion', description: 'Compare zones on both sides, including the axillae.' },
      { id: 'r7', category: 'Auscultation', title: 'Breath Sounds', description: 'Compare vesicular vs bronchial sounds and check for added sounds (crackles/wheeze).' }
    ]
  },
  {

    system: ExamSystem.ABDOMINAL,
    name: 'Abdominal Examination',

Added interactive checklist, multimedia (video embeds, step images), and progress tracking.
Integrated search and filtering logic to filter by name, system, keywords, and description.

payload.text:
Provide a list of positive and negative findings for each step of the Examination with pertinent examples/ images of positive findings where relevant.

For relevant examinations like Cardiovascular and Respiratory, integrate a basic audio simulation for auscultation sounds (e.g., heart murmurs, crackles, wheezes). This could be triggered from a new 'Audio Simulation' tab in the ExamView and would significantly enhance the practical learning experience.

**Developing the Library's Scope**

I'm focused on fleshing out the scope for the MedEx Library enhancements. I'm currently prioritizing the incorporation of positive and negative findings for each examination step, complete with illustrative examples and images. Audio simulations for auscultation are also on the agenda, starting with heart sounds.


**Expanding the Audio Component**

Now, I'm integrating audio simulations for auscultation, specifically heart and lung sounds, into a new "Audio Simulation" tab. I'm updating `types.ts` and `constants.ts` to accommodate the `findings` and `audioSimulations` data, and updating `ExamView.tsx` to display findings within protocol steps and implement an audio player within the new tab for specific auscultation sounds. The UI will feature visual elements for playback.

entries.description: Enhanced types to include clinical findings (positive/negative) and audio simulation data.
Updated EXAMINATIONS with clinical findings and audio simulation samples.
Integrated Clinical Findings (positive/negative) and Audio Simulation tab with an interactive player.

payload.text:
Add a feature to search for reliable sources to populate the examination information within the app.
This way the application can be developed without any data, and the data can be added later like individual plugins for each system.
The app should however have a complete list of the different systems and examinations. This should be structured in a very similar way to talley and O'connor

**Developing Medical Search Feature**

I'm currently focused on integrating the Gemini API for medical source searches to dynamically populate examination details. The existing app has static data, so I'm moving toward a more dynamic approach. My first step is to establish the architecture for querying reliable sources. I need to make sure the app can handle this efficiently and correctly.


**Refining Skeleton Data**

I'm now refining the `constants.ts` file to include a comprehensive skeleton of examinations, organized using the Talley & O'Connor taxonomy. I'm focusing on the systems: General, Cardiovascular, Respiratory, Gastrointestinal, Neurological, Endocrine, Rheumatological, Reproductive/Urogenital, and Pediatrics/Geriatrics. Simultaneously, I'm updating `GeminiService` to use `googleSearch` to populate examination objects with any missing details. This addresses the "fetch" or "generate" requirement for dynamic data.


**Expanding Examination Metadata**

I'm expanding the data model in `types.ts` to accommodate dynamic population. I'm adding an `isDraft` flag and making several fields optional to handle the initial minimal metadata versus the full, Gemini-populated data. I'm also preparing to update `constants.ts` with the complete Talley & O'Connor taxonomy and am currently outlining how to integrate a new `populateExamination` method in `geminiService.ts` that will use `googleSearch`. My goal is to add a dynamic data fetch method, while integrating the Gemini API to search for things like descriptions, steps, and findings.

To transform the app into a dynamic clinical repository that follows the **Talley & O’Connor** taxonomy, I will implement a "Clinical Research Engine." This feature allows the application to exist as a comprehensive skeleton that can be "populated" on-demand using Gemini’s **Google Search Grounding**.

### Specification
1.  **Comprehensive Taxonomy**: Update the database with a full list of examinations categorized by systems (CVS, Respiratory, GI, Neurological, MSK, Endocrine, etc.), mirroring the structure of major medical textbooks.
2.  **Dynamic Data Acquisition**: For examinations without hardcoded steps, the app will trigger a "Medical Research" phase. Gemini will use `googleSearch` to find reliable clinical protocols from sources like Geeky Medics, OSCEstop, and BMJ Best Practice.
3.  **Source Attribution**: Following the grounding rules, the app will display the exact URLs used to synthesize the protocol, ensuring transparency and reliability.
4.  **UI/UX**:
    *   **Researching State**: A sophisticated animation showing "Reviewing medical literature..." with a real-time log of the search process.
    *   **Skeleton Cards**: Exams not yet "downloaded" will have a distinct "Research Required" visual state.
    *   **Persistent Synthesis**: Once a protocol is generated, it remains available for the session.

### Implementation

entries.description: Add isDraft flag to Examination and a type for Grounding Sources.
Expanded Taxonomy following Talley & O'Connor structure with draft markers for dynamic content.
Add populateExamination method using Google Search grounding.
Add dynamic population logic with a research loading screen and sources display.
Add distinct visual state for Draft/Research-Ready examinations.

payload.text:
The app now functions as an "AI Medical Research Assistant." When you click on an exam labeled "Research Ready" (like Peripheral Vascular or Upper Limb Neuro), the app will not just show an empty page. Instead, it triggers a high-fidelity "Researching" phase where Gemini Pro uses Google Search to synthesize a professional protocol from established medical sources. 

This mirrors the **Talley & O'Connor** categorization while providing the same dynamic, visual-first experience of **Geeky Medics** or **OSCEstop**. Sources are clearly attributed with links at the bottom of the synthesized protocol.

diagnostics.message: Property '[ExamSystem.GENERAL]' is missing in type '{ Cardiovascular: any; Respiratory: any; Abdominal: any; Neurological: any; Musculoskeletal: any; Endocrine: any; Vascular: any; "Cranial Nerves": any; }' but required in type 'Record<ExamSystem, React.ReactNode>'.

entries.description: Add missing ExamSystem.GENERAL icon to systemIcons to satisfy the Record type requirement and fix the TypeScript error.

payload.text: Create a download Manager Tab, as well as a download queue for the app. This will be used when downloading information for examinations/ Creating images/ Creating auscultation sounds. Add Logic to use asynchronus downloads, To prevent exceeding rate limits, a robust Smart retry mechanism that we change the API request dependent on the error message returned, Drag and drop cancelling, Progress bar, Pause, Atomic saves to prevent corruption, And automatic skipping of already existing files

**Considering Download Manager Needs**

I'm focusing on building a "Download Manager" for MedEx. I've been thinking about the download queue – examinations, images, and audio. It's crucial for the process to be asynchronous, running in the background. The design must be robust!


**Implementing Download Manager Features**

I've been honing in on the UI now. I plan to incorporate progress bars, and pause/resume capabilities. I am prioritizing robust retry logic based on error messages, along with drag-and-drop to remove items from the queue. For data integrity, atomic saves are my focus, likely using IndexedDB or local storage with a safe pattern. I'm also ensuring efficiency with deduplication for existing files. I'm also thinking of how the Gemini Service will work.


**Refining Manager Architecture**

I'm solidifying the download manager's internal architecture now. I'm leaning heavily on IndexedDB for atomic saves and session persistence to prevent redundant downloads. I have also started working on the "Smart Retry" feature, which analyzes Gemini API errors for smart adjustments. I'm designing the UI to reflect a professional medical theme, and implementing a list view with task icons for streamlined navigation.

entries.description: Add types for Download Manager and task tracking.
Create a persistent storage service using IndexedDB for atomic saves and deduplication.
Core download manager logic with queueing, smart retry, and concurrency control.
UI for the Download Manager with queue management, progress tracking, and drag-and-drop cancel.
Add Download Manager to Sidebar navigation.
Integrate Download Manager view and update main layout logic.

payload.text: Implement offline support for downloaded examinations. Users should be able to access downloaded content, including text, images, and audio simulations, even when they have no internet connection, by leveraging IndexedDB for local storage.

**Considering Offline Storage**

I've made significant strides. I've successfully set up the `StorageService`, utilizing IndexedDB for efficient saving and retrieval of medical examinations, and integrated a `DownloadManagerService` to handle the population of these examinations. This creates a good framework for offline support.


**Enhancing Offline Capabilities**

I'm now focused on fleshing out the offline support. I'm updating the `StorageService` to handle blob data, enabling the storage of image and audio files as base64. The `DownloadManagerService` will be updated to fetch and convert external URLs to base64, storing them during a "Deep Sync." I'll also modify `ExamView.tsx` to detect offline status, using stored assets if available and gracefully degrading features as needed.


**Refining Offline Data Management**

I'm now integrating AI-driven feature support. I'm adding comprehensive error handling to `GeminiService` to address connectivity issues and improve the "Offline" UI state. I'm updating `Examination` and related types to handle `localUrl` or `data` fields for assets. The `DownloadManagerService` is getting an update to fetch and store remote assets as base64 in IndexedDB during a sync. I'll prioritize local assets in `ExamView`. I'm verifying that I'm able to fetch these assets across origins.

entries.description: Add fields for local asset storage to support offline access.
Implement deep asset syncing (fetching images and audio to store as base64 for offline use).
Integrate offline detection and prioritize local assets. Disable online-only features when disconnected.

payload.text:
In the ExamView component, next to the 'Open Protocol' button on exam cards in App.tsx, add a small 'Quick Sync' icon (CloudDownload) that initiates a download for that specific examination's core content (text, images) for offline viewing, without navigating to the Download Manager.

Please also create "Quick Review" Hyperlinks that will pop-up a modal showing a related examination or test to allow for rapid revision of related content. 
IE. Visual fields is a part of the cranial nerve exam, but is also part of the Visual Acuity exam. To prevent multiple instances of the same data across multiple tests, (and because visual acuity is not always performed for a Cranial nerve exam) we could instead have a note that says "Optic nerve: Assessed through Visual Fields, Ishihara, Schnellen Chart, Confrontation" that are hyperlinks to each of those sections in the relevant eye examination.

**Considering Feature Implementation**

I'm focusing on incorporating a CloudDownload icon adjacent to the "Open Protocol" button in `App.tsx` for quick content downloads. The goal is a seamless user experience, avoiding page reloads. I'm also thinking about implementing hyperlinks within the exam steps for enhanced navigation.


**Implementing Quick Review Links**

I'm now integrating a "Quick Review" modal to avoid data redundancy and connect related concepts within exam steps. I'll be updating the `Examination` type and implementing the logic in `App.tsx` and `ExamView.tsx`. The "Quick Sync" icon will also be added to exam cards. I'm focusing on defining hyperlinks within exam steps, and thinking about either a regex-based approach on a specific format in descriptions or post-processing on the user's suggestions.


**Defining Related Exam IDs**

I'm expanding the functionality for the "Quick Review" modal. I've updated `types.ts` to include `relatedExamIds` in the `Examination` type and will populate this in `constants.ts` with some sample related exam data. The `App.tsx` file is next, where I'll add the Quick Sync icon and logic to trigger the modal. I'm focusing on text-to-hyperlink conversion within `ExamView.tsx`, aiming for an elegant solution to link concepts, and have drafted the `QuickReviewModal` component. I need to ensure a professional look for the new elements.

entries.description: Add relatedExamIds to Examination type to support Quick Review links.
Add related links for Cranial Nerves and Visual Acuity to demonstrate Quick Review functionality.


**Diagnosing The Error**

Okay, I'm currently focused on this pesky 500 RPC error. It seems to be related to the Gemini API, specifically during the protocol synthesis. I'm digging into potential causes like connection issues, invalid API keys, or even rate limits on my end. I'm exploring offline support conflicts as well, and will keep you posted on my progress!


**Pinpointing The Mismatch**

Okay, I've identified a discrepancy. While the coding guidelines mandate `gemini-3-flash-preview` or `gemini-3-pro-preview`, the `populateExamination` function in `geminiService.ts` is explicitly using `gemini-3-pro-preview`. The user prompt indicates "Download failed", which occurs within `runTask` in `downloadManagerService.ts`. This triggers `gemini.populateExamination`. Also, that `Rpc failed due to xhr error... error code: 6` is a strong indicator of model mismatches, or backend server trouble.


**Standardizing Model Calls**

I'm now focusing on standardizing the model calls, as suggested, to `gemini-3-flash-preview`. The earlier error code 6 points towards a potential configuration or backend issue with experimental models. Currently, I'm verifying the calls in `GeminiService.ts` to ensure consistency.


**Refining Error Source**

I'm now zeroing in on the "Rpc failed" error, which seems linked to the internal proxy, suggesting possible instability with specific models or generation configurations. I'm re-examining `populateExamination`, which uses `gemini-3-pro-preview` with `googleSearch`. The model naming conventions are clear: `gemini-3-flash-preview` for basic and `gemini-3-pro-preview` for complex tasks. I'm also now scrutinizing the `responseSchema`, as per guidelines.


**Streamlining Model Usage**

I'm now streamlining service calls and adhering strictly to the `generateContent` method, bypassing any initial model definitions. I verified `GeminiService.ts` follows the correct pattern. To improve stability, I'm testing `gemini-3-flash-preview` in `populateExamination`, swapping it for `gemini-3-pro-preview`. The "code: 6" error is still puzzling, but could be transient issues with `gemini-3-pro-preview`, so I'll standardize. I'm updating `GeminiService.ts` to use `gemini-3-flash-preview` consistently.


**Enhancing Stability and Clarity**

I'm now focusing on stabilizing and clarifying calls to maximize performance for the experimental preview models. I'm also refining `populateExamination` to be more resilient and ensure that the prompt explicitly outlines the JSON structure. Additionally, I'm now double-checking all `googleSearch` parameters to ensure they're accurate. My priority remains the RPC error fix. I have also verified that the provided file content, which *includes* previous changes such as a more compact layout, is up to date, and am continuing to verify the layout refinements.


**Optimizing Synthesis Performance**

I'm now investigating how to improve synthesis task performance. I'll switch to `gemini-3-flash-preview` for synthesis, as it's faster and less prone to timeout errors, and should address the 500 RPC error. I'm also setting `thinkingBudget: 0` to disable the thinking process. I am examining the provided `DownloadManager.tsx` to check for layout updates, and found the prior updates were correctly implemented.

payload.text:
In the DownloadManager component, add a 'Retry' button next to failed download tasks. This button should allow users to re-initiate the download for individual failed items.

Ensure api calls for Images and text are treated as separate calls to prevent type or format errors in the returned response

**Considering Download Retries**

I'm focusing on adding a retry mechanism to the `DownloadManager` for failed tasks, making it a simple button. I'm also thinking through separating API calls, especially for images and text, to optimize how we download the content. I'm thinking about how the `gemini.populateExamination` function ties into this separation, it needs a bit more work.


**Refining Download Strategies**

I'm now refining the `DownloadManagerService` and `GeminiService` to create discrete steps for content fetching. The goal is to first retrieve protocol text and then handle individual assets, like images, separately. This approach addresses the user's concern about combining image analysis/generation requests with the initial text retrieval, avoiding potential format errors. This aims to keep requests smaller and prevent RPC failures.


**Revising API Call Granularity**

I'm now focusing on separating the `GeminiService`'s calls for images and text more explicitly. Initially, I'll revise `populateExamination` to exclusively handle text-based content, ensuring the current flow is robust. Then, I may add a follow-up step to enrich the text with image data, if needed. I'm also considering creating separate `populateSteps` and `populateFindings` methods for finer-grained control.


**Prioritizing Retry Implementation**

I'm now implementing the Retry button for failed downloads in `DownloadManager`. The `retryTask(taskId)` function will reset the status to pending, reset the retry count, and clear the error. The UI will have a refresh icon/button for failed tasks. I'm prioritizing this before separating API calls further. The user's feedback makes it clear to keep things simple with the retry process.



**Verifying Functionality Design**

I'm now diving into the implications of `googleSearch` with `application/json`, since that has caused problems. I'll verify the structure of the `GeminiService` to ensure this is handled well. I also will make the `populateExamination` prompt as simple as possible. I'll also implement the Retry button and the split logic for now.


**Structuring AI Enrichment**

I'm now focusing on a new method, `GeminiService.enrichClinicalFinding`, to handle enriching the text-based findings with further detail. This uses the current `populateExamination` as the text call and avoids handling image data in the text synthesis. This isolates the content-fetching calls into discrete steps, especially for any potential future image analysis from models like `gemini-2.5-flash-image`, as the user mentioned. The `runTask` will then include a step to call the enrich function.

payload.text:
In the DownloadManager component, add a visual progress bar for each task showing the download percentage.


Here is also information regarding the Examinations:

Comprehensive Taxonomic Compendium of Clinical Medical Examinations: A Collegiate Learning FrameworkThe clinical examination is the structural cornerstone of diagnostic medicine, representing a sophisticated synthesis of anatomical knowledge, physiological interpretation, and interpersonal communication. While the twenty-first century has seen an unprecedented expansion in diagnostic imaging and laboratory biomarkers, the bedside assessment remains the primary tool for diagnostic hypothesis generation and the establishment of a therapeutic alliance. This report provides an exhaustive, taxonomically organized compendium of clinical examinations, structured to align with the collegiate standards of the Royal Australasian College of Physicians (RACP) and the Australian Medical Council (AMC), utilizing the organizational hierarchy found in seminal texts like Talley and O'Connor’s Clinical Examination.The General Principles of Clinical AssessmentThe efficacy of any clinical examination is predicated upon the preliminary interaction between the clinician and the patient. This phase involves not only data collection but the navigation of ethical and social complexities, particularly in the context of cultural diversity and the varying needs of patients across the lifespan. The collegiate standard emphasizes a patient-centric approach that integrates evidence-based medicine into every physical maneuver.The foundation of the clinical encounter is the "WIIPPPPE" framework, which ensures safety, privacy, and clinical rigor. This includes Washing hands (infection control), Introducing oneself and the role, identifying the patient via two identifiers, obtaining explicit Permission (informed consent), inquiring about Pain prior to palpation, ensuring Privacy (curtains and chaperones), and achieving adequate Exposure while maintaining patient dignity.Preliminary Observations and Vital SignsBefore a systems-based examination commences, the clinician performs a general inspection to assess the "end of the bed" status of the patient. This includes observing for signs of acute distress, cachexia, nutritional status, and the presence of medical adjuncts such as intravenous lines, oxygen masks, or drainage bags.The measurement of vital signs provides a quantitative physiological baseline. This triad of pulse, respiration, and blood pressure, augmented by temperature and oxygen saturation, informs the urgency of the clinical situation. Assessment of the arterial pulse involves analyzing the rate, rhythm, volume, and character, alongside checks for radio-radial and radio-femoral delay to screen for aortic coarctation or dissection. Blood pressure must be measured using an appropriate cuff size, with postural checks (lying and standing) performed to identify orthostatic hypotension, defined as a drop of $>20$ mmHg systolic or $>10$ mmHg diastolic upon standing.Facies and Systemic SignsGeneral inspection extends to the patient's facies, which may reveal pathognomonic features of systemic disease. For example, the presence of malar flush suggests mitral stenosis, while the "moon facies" of Cushing’s syndrome or the exophthalmos of Graves’ disease provide immediate diagnostic clues. The clinician must also assess for:SignClinical AssociationMechanismJaundiceHyperbilirubinaemiaLiver disease, hemolysis, or biliary obstruction CyanosisDeoxygenated hemoglobinCentral (cardiac/respiratory) or peripheral (vasoconstriction) PallorAnemia or vasoconstrictionReduction in cutaneous blood flow or hemoglobin levels ClubbingChronic hypoxia/inflammationDistal digital hypertrophy related to various lung/heart diseases HydrationFluid volume statusAssessment of mucous membranes, skin turgor, and fontanelles (in neonates) The Cardiovascular SystemThe cardiovascular examination is a rigorous process designed to evaluate the heart's mechanical pump function and the integrity of the vascular tree. It is divided into the central precordial examination and the peripheral vascular assessment.The Cardiovascular HistoryA comprehensive history is the precursor to the physical exam, focusing on cardinal symptoms: chest pain, dyspnoea (orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea), palpitations, syncope, and claudication. The SOCRATES acronym (Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Associations, Timing, Exacerbating/Relieving factors, Severity) is used to analyze pain, particularly to differentiate myocardial ischemia from pericarditic or pleuritic pain.The Precordial ExaminationThe examination of the precordium follows a sequence of inspection, palpation, and auscultation. Percussion is rarely used in modern cardiology but may be employed to identify a grossly enlarged heart or pericardial effusion.Inspection identifies surgical scars, visible pulsations, and chest wall deformities such as pectus carinatum or excavatum. Palpation is directed at locating the apex beat—the lowest, most lateral point of palpable cardiac impulse—normally found in the 5th intercostal space in the mid-clavicular line. Displacement suggests ventricular enlargement or mediastinal shift. The clinician also palpates for "heaves" (suggestive of ventricular hypertrophy) and "thrills" (palpable murmurs) using the bony part of the palm.Auscultation remains the most technically demanding skill, requiring the identification of the four primary valves. The clinician uses both the diaphragm (for high-pitched sounds like $S_1$, $S_2$, and most murmurs) and the bell (for low-pitched sounds like $S_3$ and $S_4$) of the stethoscope.Heart Sound/MurmurTimingSignificanceFirst Heart Sound ($S_1$)Start of systoleClosure of mitral and tricuspid valves Second Heart Sound ($S_2$)Start of diastoleClosure of aortic and pulmonary valves Third Heart Sound ($S_3$)Early diastoleRapid ventricular filling (normal in young; suggests failure in elderly) Fourth Heart Sound ($S_4$)Late diastoleAtrial contraction into a stiff ventricle (e.g., hypertrophy) Systolic MurmurBetween $S_1$ and $S_2$Stenosis of outflow valves or regurgitation of AV valves Diastolic MurmurBetween $S_2$ and $S_1$Regurgitation of outflow valves or stenosis of AV valves The Jugular Venous Pressure (JVP)The JVP is a vital non-invasive clinical "window" into the right atrium. It is measured at the internal jugular vein, which is medial to the sternocleidomastoid muscle. The clinician assesses the vertical height of the venous pulsation above the sternal angle; a height $>3$ cm is considered elevated. The waveform is characterized by the 'a' wave (atrial contraction), 'v' wave (venous filling), and 'x'/'y' descents. Abnormalities such as giant 'a' waves (tricuspid stenosis) or 'cannon' waves (AV dissociation) provide critical diagnostic information.The Respiratory SystemThe respiratory examination assesses the efficiency of gas exchange and the integrity of the pulmonary parenchyma, pleura, and chest wall.The Respiratory HistoryKey symptoms include cough (noting sputum color/consistency), haemoptysis, wheeze, and chest pain. Occupational history is particularly relevant, screening for exposure to asbestos, coal dust, or silica, which can lead to restrictive lung disease.Physical Examination of the ChestThe physical examination is traditionally performed with the patient sitting upright, following the sequence of inspection, palpation, percussion, and auscultation.Inspection looks for the "barrel chest" of emphysema, the use of accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, scalenes), and intercostal recession. Palpation evaluates the position of the trachea—normally midline—which may be pulled toward a collapsed lung or pushed away by a tension pneumothorax or large pleural effusion. Chest expansion is measured by placing the hands on the patient's back with thumbs meeting at the midline; a symmetric movement of at least 3-5 cm is expected.Percussion involves tapping the middle finger of one hand against the other, placed firmly on the chest wall. Resonance is normal; "dullness" indicates fluid or consolidation, while "stony dullness" is pathognomonic for a pleural effusion. Auscultation classifies breath sounds into vesicular (normal) and bronchial (tubular and high-pitched, occurring over areas of consolidation). Added sounds include:Crackles (Crepitations): Non-musical, discontinuous sounds often indicating fluid in the alveoli (pulmonary edema) or fibrosis.Wheeze: Musical, continuous sounds usually associated with narrowed airways (asthma or COPD).Pleural Rub: A creaking sound caused by the friction of inflamed pleural surfaces.Vocal resonance is assessed by asking the patient to whisper "99" while auscultating; increased resonance suggests consolidation.The Gastrointestinal SystemThe abdominal examination evaluates the gastrointestinal tract and the accessory organs (liver, gallbladder, spleen, and pancreas). It requires the patient to be lying supine with the abdomen fully exposed from the xiphisternum to the symphysis pubis.The Gastrointestinal HistoryHistory taking centers on abdominal pain, changes in bowel habit (constipation, diarrhea), jaundice, dysphagia, and weight loss. The presence of hematemesis or melena indicates an acute upper gastrointestinal bleed, requiring urgent intervention.Examination of the AbdomenThe sequence for the abdomen is inspection, palpation, percussion, and auscultation.Inspection observes for distension (the 5 F's: Fat, Fluid, Flatus, Feces, Fetus), surgical scars, striae, and the caput medusae of portal hypertension. Palpation begins with light touch in all nine regions to identify tenderness or guarding (muscular contraction over an area of inflammation). Deep palpation seeks to identify organomegaly or masses.The liver is palpated by starting in the right iliac fossa and moving toward the right costal margin. A normal liver edge may be felt just below the margin during deep inspiration; a palpable liver $>2$ cm below the margin suggests hepatomegaly. The spleen is palpated by moving from the right iliac fossa diagonally toward the left costal margin; it is usually only palpable when enlarged to twice its normal size.Percussion is used to define the liver span and to detect ascites through "shifting dullness." In this maneuver, the clinician percusses from the umbilicus to the flank; if dullness is reached, the patient rolls toward the examiner, and the clinician waits to see if the area becomes resonant as the fluid shifts. Auscultation assesses bowel sounds (absent in ileus or peritonitis; "tinkling" in obstruction) and searches for bruits over the renal and iliac arteries.The Nervous SystemThe neurological examination is arguably the most comprehensive component of clinical assessment, requiring a systematic evaluation of the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.The Neurological HistoryHistory taking focuses on sensory or motor deficits, headaches, seizures, visual disturbances, and cognitive changes. The clinician must determine the speed of onset (vascular vs. inflammatory vs. neoplastic) and the localization of the lesion.Cranial Nerve ExaminationThe twelve pairs of cranial nerves are tested to identify brainstem or localized skull base pathology.NerveExamination MethodClinical SignificanceCN I (Olfactory)Odor identification (e.g., coffee, peppermint)Anosmia (trauma, neurodegeneration) CN II (Optic)Acuity, Fields, Reflexes, FundoscopyVisual loss, papilledema CN III, IV, VIExtraocular movements (the "H" test)Diplopia, ophthalmoplegia CN V (Trigeminal)Facial sensation, corneal reflex, jaw strengthSensory loss, trigeminal neuralgia CN VII (Facial)Smile, frown, puff cheeks, eye closureBell’s Palsy vs. Upper Motor Neuron lesion CN VIII (Vestib.)Whisper test, Weber and Rinne testsConductive vs. sensorineural hearing loss CN IX, X (Glosso/Vagus)Palatal rise, gag reflex, swallowingDysphagia, vocal cord paralysis CN XI (Accessory)Shrug shoulders, head turn against resistanceTrapezius/Sternocleidomastoid weakness CN XII (Hypoglossal)Tongue protrusion, inspection for wastingFasciculations, deviation to side of lesion The Motor SystemMotor assessment involves a hierarchy of observation and testing:Inspection: Wasting (atrophy), fasciculations (muscle twitches), and involuntary movements (tremors, tics).Tone: Passive movement of joints to identify spasticity (velocity-dependent resistance) or rigidity (lead-pipe or cogwheel).Power: Graded on the Medical Research Council (MRC) scale from 0 (no contraction) to 5 (normal strength). Testing is done proximally and distally to localize the level of nerve or spinal root involvement.Reflexes: Deep tendon reflexes (Biceps C5/C6, Triceps C7/C8, Knee L3/L4, Ankle S1) are graded as absent, normal, or brisk. The plantar reflex is checked for the Babinski sign (upgoing toe), indicating an upper motor neuron lesion.Coordination: Assessed via the finger-nose test (for upper limbs) and heel-shin test (for lower limbs) to screen for cerebellar ataxia.The Sensory System and GaitSensory examination maps the dermatomes and evaluates different neural pathways. Light touch and proprioception (position sense) assess the dorsal columns, while pain (pinprick) and temperature assess the spinothalamic tracts. Vibration sense is tested using a 128 Hz tuning fork on bony prominences.Gait analysis concludes the examination, looking for pathognomonic patterns:Hemiplegic: Circumduction of the leg and a flexed arm.Parkinsonian: Shuffling, short steps, and loss of arm swing.Ataxic: Wide-based and unsteady.Antalgic: Shortened stance phase on the painful limb.Mental State Examination (MSE)The MSE is a structured assessment of the patient's psychological and cognitive status at the time of the interview. It is essential in psychiatry, neurology, and geriatrics.Structure of the MSEThe examination evaluates several distinct domains through observation and direct questioning.Appearance and Behavior: Grooming, hygiene, facial expression, and level of consciousness (assessed from alert to comatose using Plum and Posner’s definitions).Speech: Evaluation of rate, rhythm, volume, and fluency. Dysarthria (motor speech issue) or aphasia (language processing issue) may be identified.Mood and Affect: Mood is the internal emotional climate; affect is the outward, observable emotional expression (described as flat, blunted, or labile).Thought Process and Content: "Process" refers to the logical flow (flight of ideas, loosening of associations), while "content" refers to what the patient is thinking, including delusions, obsessions, or suicidal/homicidal ideation.Perceptions: Screening for hallucinations (perceiving stimuli that are not there) or illusions (misinterpreting real stimuli).Cognition: Assessment of orientation (Time, Place, Person), attention (serial 7s), and memory (immediate, recent, and remote recall).Insight and Judgment: Does the patient understand they are ill, and can they make safe decisions about their care?.The Musculoskeletal SystemMusculoskeletal examinations focus on the joints, muscles, and connective tissues. They are typically performed using the "Look, Feel, Move, Special Tests" sequence.Screening and Regional ExaminationsThe GALS screen (Gait, Arms, Legs, Spine) serves as a rapid preliminary assessment for any locomotor dysfunction.For regional joint exams, inspection identifies redness, swelling, deformity (such as the ulnar deviation in rheumatoid arthritis), or wasting of muscle bulk (e.g., thenar eminence wasting in carpal tunnel syndrome). Palpation identifies local warmth, tenderness, and joint effusions. Movement is assessed both actively (by the patient) and passively (by the examiner), noting the range of motion and the presence of crepitus.Orthopaedic Special TestsThese maneuvers are designed to stress specific structures to identify localized pathology.JointTestTarget StructurePositive FindingKneeMcMurray’sMeniscusPalpable/audible click or pain KneeLachman’sAnterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL)Increased anterior translation, soft end-point KneeValgus/Varus StressMCL/LCLJoint line opening at 0° and 30° flexion WristPhalen’sMedian NerveParesthesia in the first 3.5 fingers HipTrendelenburgAbductor Muscles (Gluteus Medius)Pelvis drops toward the unsupported side ShoulderEmpty Can TestSupraspinatus TendonWeakness or pain during resisted abduction The Haematological and Endocrine SystemsThese systems often manifest through multisystem signs that require a "global" examination approach.Haematological ExaminationAssessment involves screening for anemia (conjunctival pallor), thrombocytopenia (petechiae and purpura), and lymphadenopathy. Lymph nodes are palpated in a specific sequence: submental, submandibular, pre-auricular, post-auricular, occipital, cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, and inguinal. The clinician also checks for hepatosplenomegaly, which may indicate a lymphoproliferative disorder.Endocrine ExaminationSpecific focus is given to the thyroid gland and the metabolic effects of hormones.Thyroid inspection requires the patient to drink water; the thyroid moves upward on swallowing. The clinician palpates the gland from behind the patient, assessing for size, consistency, and nodules. Diabetic foot examination is a specialized endocrine task involving the assessment of pulses, skin integrity, and sensation (using a 10g monofilament and 128 Hz tuning fork) to screen for peripheral neuropathy and vascular insufficiency.Specialty Clinical ExaminationsOphthalmology (The 8-Point Exam)A thorough eye exam is critical for both primary ocular conditions and systemic diseases like diabetes and hypertension.Visual Acuity: Recorded for each eye using a Snellen chart (for distance) or a near card.Pupils: Assessment for anisocoria (unequal size) and the Relative Afferent Pupillary Defect (RAPD) using the swinging flashlight test.Extraocular Motility: Assessing for cranial nerve palsies or muscle entrapment.Intraocular Pressure (IOP): Measured via tonometry (Goldmann applanation is the gold standard).Confrontation Visual Fields: Mapping peripheral vision in four quadrants.External Examination: Evaluating the lids, lacrimal system, and orbit for ptosis, proptosis, or lesions.Slit-lamp Examination: A microscopic view of the anterior segment, including the cornea and anterior chamber (looking for "flare" or "cells").Fundoscopy: Evaluation of the optic nerve head (disc), macula, and peripheral retina.Ear, Nose, and Throat (ENT)The ENT exam combines visual inspection with functional testing.Otoscopy evaluates the external auditory canal and the tympanic membrane (TM). A healthy TM is pearly-gray and translucent, with a visible "cone of light". Hearing is assessed via the whisper test and tuning fork maneuvers:Weber Test: A 512 Hz fork is placed on the midline of the skull. Sound lateralizes to the side of a conductive loss or away from a sensorineural loss.Rinne Test: Compares air conduction (AC) to bone conduction (BC). Normal (Rinne positive) is $AC > BC$. If $BC > AC$, it indicates conductive hearing loss.Nasal examination identifies septal deviation, polyps, or inflamed turbinates. The oral cavity exam focuses on the tongue, palate, and tonsils (graded 1+ to 4+ based on oropharyngeal obstruction).DermatologyDermatological examination requires systematic inspection of the entire skin surface, hair, and nails. Lesions are described by their morphology:Macule/Patch: Flat area of color change ($<1$ cm / $>1$ cm).Papule/Plaque: Raised, solid lesion ($<1$ cm / $>1$ cm).Vesicle/Bulla: Fluid-filled lesion ($<1$ cm / $>1$ cm).The ABCDE and 7-point checklists are used for suspicious pigmented lesions to screen for melanoma.FeatureABCDE System7-Point Checklist (Major Criteria = 2 pts, Minor = 1 pt)AAsymmetryChange in size (Major) BBorder irregularityChange in shape (Major) CColor variegationChange in color (Major) DDiameter $>6$ mmInflammation (Minor) EEvolving over timeCrusting/Bleeding (Minor); Sensory change (Minor); Diameter $\geq 7$ mm (Minor) A score of $\geq 3$ on the 7-point checklist necessitates urgent referral to a dermatologist.Paediatrics and Neonatal ExaminationPaediatric clinical assessment requires a nuanced understanding of development and the use of age-appropriate techniques.The Neonatal ExaminationThe first assessment occurs at birth with the Apgar score, which evaluates the infant's physiological adaptation at 1 and 5 minutes.Sign012Heart RateAbsent$<100$$>100$Respiratory EffortAbsentWeak/IrregularGood/CryingMuscle ToneFlaccidSome FlexionActive MovementReflex IrritabilityNo ResponseGrimaceGood CryColorBlue/PaleAcrocyanosisPink All OverA score of 7-10 is normal, while $<7$ may require resuscitation. The newborn physical exam also screens for congenital anomalies, checks the patency of the anus, and evaluates the fontanelles (soft spots) for bulging (indicating raised intracranial pressure) or depression (indicating dehydration).Developmental MilestonesTracking milestones is the hallmark of pediatric practice, ensuring children meet gross motor, fine motor, language, and social targets.2 Months: Social smile, cooing, holds head up on tummy.6 Months: Sits with support, babbles, rolls tummy to back.9 Months: Separation anxiety, crawls, pincer grasp begins.12 Months: Walks with support, says "mama" or "dada," follows simple commands.2 Years: Parallel play, 2-word sentences, kicks a ball.Women’s Health: Gynaecology and ObstetricsThese examinations require high degrees of technical skill and professional etiquette, including the use of a chaperone and explicit consent at every step.Gynaecological and Breast ExaminationBreast examination involves inspection (looking for skin dimpling, nipple inversion, or peau d'orange) and systematic palpation of the four quadrants and the axillary tail (Tail of Spence).The pelvic examination includes:Inspection: Checking the external genitalia for lesions, discharge, or prolapse.Speculum Exam: Insertion of a Graves or Cusco speculum to visualize the cervix and take cytological samples.Bimanual Exam: Palpation of the uterus and adnexa between the internal fingers and the external hand to assess size, position, and tenderness (e.g., cervical motion tenderness in pelvic inflammatory disease).Obstetric ExaminationIn the pregnant patient, the abdominal exam focuses on fetal well-being. Fundal height is measured to assess growth. Leopold's maneuvers are performed to determine the fetal position (longitudinal vs. transverse) and the presentation (cephalic vs. breech). Fetal heart rate is auscultated, and the clinician screens for complications such as pre-eclampsia (hypertension, edema, and proteinuria).The Geriatric Patient and Acute CareGeriatric AssessmentThe comprehensive geriatric assessment focuses on "functional age" rather than chronological age. This includes the evaluation of:Activities of Daily Living (ADLs): Feeding, dressing, bathing, and mobility.Polypharmacy: Reviewing all medications to reduce the risk of adverse drug interactions.Cognitive Screening: Assessment of memory and executive function to screen for dementia or delirium.The Acutely Ill PatientIn emergency scenarios, the clinician adopts the ABCDE approach (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) to stabilize the patient before a definitive diagnosis is sought. The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is used to quantify the level of consciousness based on Eye (1-4), Verbal (1-5), and Motor (1-6) responses. A GCS $\leq 8$ indicates a significant risk to the airway.Synthesis and Clinical ReasoningThe list of clinical examinations provided here represents the breadth of medical diagnostics. However, the true mastery of clinical examination lies in the synthesis of physical signs into a coherent differential diagnosis. As medical students and trainees transition to professional practice, they must learn to perform "focused" examinations—selecting only the maneuvers relevant to the patient's presenting problem—while maintaining the technical rigor expected by colleges such as the RACP and AMC. The integration of these skills into a digital learning database will serve as a foundational resource for the next generation of evidence-based, patient-centric practitioners.

**Visualizing Download Progress**

I'm currently focused on integrating progress bars into the `DownloadManager`. The goal is to provide a clear visual representation of download percentages for each task. I'm also considering how to differentiate between API calls for image and text data. My primary concern is to ensure a user-friendly and informative interface for download monitoring.


**Refining Progress Visualization**

I'm now refining the visual progress indicators in `DownloadManager.tsx`. I noticed an existing progress bar, but I need to make it more prominent and ensure its accuracy within the 0-100% range. I am also planning to improve the user interface for monitoring the download tasks. I'll need to figure out how to differentiate API calls for image and text data, as previously requested, to show a more robust process that's easier to follow.


**Separating Fetch Calls**

I'm now separating text generation from asset fetching more thoroughly within `downloadManagerService.ts`. I'm refining the `runTask` logic to handle image failures gracefully, preventing them from halting the entire download process. I'm also preparing to add examinations to `constants.ts` to improve the "realness" of the library.


**Categorizing Exam Information**

I'm currently updating `constants.ts` with a wider range of examinations, like Cranial Nerves and MSE, to improve categorization. I'm also ensuring that prompts in `geminiService.ts` are strictly text-focused to keep the API focused on text generation. I've started refining the visual progress bar in `DownloadManager.tsx` with a shimmer effect and a numeric percentage indicator. I'm also separating the fetching and text generation calls in `runTask` to avoid transferring binary data to Gemini. Finally, I'm examining the scaling issues in `App.tsx`.


**Adjusting Font Sizes**

I'm now focusing on scaling issues in `App.tsx`, specifically using smaller text classes like `text-sm` and `text-xs` to improve the visual layout. I need to verify that I'm adjusting the correct container. I've confirmed that the inclusion of `"camera"` permission in `metadata.json` is appropriate, considering the user's mention of camera-related exams like "Fundoscopy" and "Slit-lamp," even though the app is a library. I'm also ensuring the categorization in `constants.ts` includes Gastro, CNS, MSE, GALS and Eye Exam. The pipeline in `downloadManagerService.ts` for text generation is working well.

payload.text:
I have reverted the CSS `scale` transform and instead implemented a high-density layout. 

### Core Changes:
- **UI Density:** Significantly reduced paddings (`p-10` to `p-5`), margins, and component dimensions across the entire application to create a professional "dashboard" feel.
- **Typography:** Scaled down font sizes (e.g., heading text from `text-9xl` to `text-4xl`, body text to `text-xs` and `text-sm`) while keeping the heavy font weights for readability.
- **Navigation:** Compacted the sidebar to `w-60` with smaller icons and labels.
- **Protocol Library:** Reduced card size and border radii for better information density.
- **Exam View:** Optimized the hero section and tab navigation to allow more protocol steps to be visible on-screen simultaneously.
- **Aesthetics:** Maintained the vibrant glassmorphism and neon borders you requested, but scaled the glows and border widths to match the new, smaller proportions.

The application now feels expansive and professional without requiring browser zooming or CSS scaling.

This is a request from the perspective of Code practice....
Can you please modularise all of the code files? Please decouple the logic and components from the UI, Please also Decouple the functionality to use SOLID and DRY principles, with each module being responsible for a single thing.

**Considering Modularization Principles**

I'm focusing on breaking down the MedEx Library code. The aim is modularity, adhering to SOLID and DRY principles. I'm prioritizing decoupling UI from logic and ensuring each module has a single responsibility. This is where I am right now.


**Analyzing Current Structure**

I've been analyzing the application's current structure in more depth, and I've started to categorize the files. I see definite areas for improvement in modularization: `App.tsx` and `ExamView.tsx` both manage styling and state which is a violation of separation of concerns. Additionally, some components handle API calls directly, violating the idea of a service layer.


**Refactoring for Clarity**

I'm now diving into the refactoring specifics. I plan to use hooks to manage data fetching and business logic. I'll centralize visual styles within a `theme.ts` file. UI components are being split into smaller reusable ones. I'm separating system-level container logic from individual exam card components, resulting in five hooks for managing state and synchronization.


**Implementing the Changes**

I'm now implementing the refactored modules. I'm focusing on the component and hook structure, and separating them as much as possible. I'm focusing on the UI components and system hooks as well as the examination hooks. I have created components for the exam card, system sections, and a tab component, and I am using hooks to manage state. I think these new structures will work.


**Defining New Modules**

I'm now identifying the new modules to create. My goal is to extract the existing code's functionality into distinct, manageable parts. I'm focusing on defining a theme file, new hook files for examinations and downloads, and common components like Badges and Cards for reuse, and I have identified some new files and services, some of which are already existing, like the Gemini and storage services.


**Defining New Structures**

I'm now defining the structure of the new modules and their responsibilities in detail. I'm focusing on UI components and system hooks as well as the examination hooks. I am creating components such as `Sidebar`, `ExaminationCard`, `SystemSection`, and elements for `ExamView`, including `Header`, `Hero`, and `TabContent`. I'm also refactoring `App.tsx` and `ExamView.tsx`. I am moving `systemStyles` to `theme.ts` and moving filtering logic and system expansion state to a hook. I'll provide standard `GlassCard` and `NeonButton` components. Finally, I've created `hooks/useExaminationState.ts` to manage search, grouping, and selection logic, and a `Layout.tsx` component.


**Organizing Components**

I'm now focusing on a more granular component breakdown. I will be handling the status of downloads using `hooks/useDownloadManager.ts`. UI is being handled by `components/ui/SharedUI.tsx` for shared glass and neon components. I am refactoring `App.tsx` and `components/ExamView.tsx`, and I'm adding `components/ExamView/StepList.tsx` for individual steps. Then, I am going to consolidate all of these into separate theme and hook files. I am going to refine my structure.

To modularize the MedEx Library according to SOLID and DRY principles, I have decoupled the application into several distinct layers:

1.  **Theme Layer (`theme.ts`)**: Centralizes all visual styles, colors, and iconography for the medical systems to ensure stylistic consistency and easy global updates.
2.  **Logic Layer (Hooks)**:
    *   `useExaminations.ts`: Manages search, filtering, grouping, and selection logic.
    *   `useDownloadStatus.ts`: Handles the real-time synchronization between the UI and the persistent background download service.
    *   `useClinicalInsights.ts`: Manages AI-powered data fetching (Gemini) and the interactive mock OSCE state.
3.  **Component Layer**: Refactored the monolithic views into single-responsibility components:
    *   `SystemSection` & `ExaminationCard`: Handles the library dashboard.
    *   `ExamView`: Acts as an orchestrator for several sub-components like `HeroSection`, `TabNavigation`, and `ProtocolAccordion`.
4.  **UI Layer**: Created reusable glassmorphic components to maintain the high-density clinical dashboard aesthetic while reducing code duplication.

entries.description: Centralized UI theme configuration for medical systems to follow DRY principles.

**Addressing TypeScript Errors**

I'm currently focused on resolving the TypeScript errors in the React application. Specifically, I'm examining `components/Sidebar.tsx`, particularly line 49. It appears to be related to the `downloadManager.subscribe` callback and how `tasks` is being used with `setActiveDownloads`.


**Fixing Effect Return Types**

I've isolated a type error within `components/Sidebar.tsx` on line 49. The `downloadManager.subscribe` callback's cleanup function, which, as implemented in `downloadManagerService.ts`, currently returns a boolean. React's `useEffect` expects `void` or a `Destructor`. The fix requires wrapping the cleanup function to ensure it returns void. This pattern is repeated in `components/DownloadManager.tsx` on line 25; a similar fix is required there as well.


**Analyzing Theme Errors**

I'm now investigating errors within `theme.ts`, specifically lines 26, 41, etc. It seems the JSX syntax for the icon components isn't being correctly parsed, likely because the file extension is `.ts` instead of `.tsx`. The suggestions I can use are limited to modifying the contents. I'll focus on converting the JSX into valid TypeScript format. I am making use of the `Activity` component here, but will look into whether I can inline it.


**Refactoring Theme Component**

I've been working to resolve the errors in `theme.ts`. I was attempting to resolve the icon rendering issues. I've switched the JSX syntax to use `React.createElement`. This change should make the TypeScript compiler happy. Now I believe I can move on to other issues that may occur, as this particular one has been addressed.

**Addressing `useEffect` in Hook**

I've also just discovered an error in `hooks/useDownloadStatus.ts`, specifically on line 15. It mirrors the `useEffect` issue I've seen previously. The `downloadManager.subscribe`'s cleanup function, which is named `unsub` in this case, needs to be wrapped to return `void`. I'll implement this fix to align it with the now-familiar pattern.


**Revisiting Previous Fixes**

I'm now revisiting all of the recent fixes across the codebase. I'm focusing on lines 49 in `components/Sidebar.tsx`, line 25 in `components/DownloadManager.tsx`, and line 15 in `hooks/useDownloadStatus.ts` to examine the fixes implemented for the `useEffect` cleanup functions. They all share the same issue, I can now see how each one has been resolved by returning `void` for the cleanup function. I'm also reviewing `theme.ts`, but this file has no updates.


**Confirming Component Properties**

I'm now confirming the component properties after changing the JSX syntax to use `React.createElement` in `theme.ts`. My initial assessment that the parsing error would be resolved appears correct. The `SystemStyle` interface in `theme.ts` still holds the correct structure with `icon: React.ReactNode`. I've also verified the `geminiService.ts` file, and confirmed the `model` property is up to date, even though this wasn't an error.


**Validating Gemini API Usage**

I'm verifying the Gemini API calls. I've confirmed key configurations like `apiKey`, `ai.models.generateContent`, and response handling are correct. The `thinkingBudget: 0` and `tools: [{ googleSearch: {} }]` settings align with the configuration. **Clarifying Tool Usage**

I'm now clarifying tool usage with specific models. I've confirmed that `gemini-3-flash-preview` is acceptable for text-based search grounding, despite being in the image generation section, and that the `googleSearch` tool is available to `gemini-3-pro-image-preview`. I am revisiting the error at `components/Sidebar.tsx` line 49 and `components/DownloadManager.tsx` line 25 to verify cleanup functions are returning `void`.



**Confirming `useEffect` Cleanup**

I've confirmed that the fix for the `useEffect` cleanup function on line 15 in `hooks/useDownloadStatus.ts` returns `void`. I've verified that the fix has been applied to this file. I'm now certain this error is resolved, as the pattern is consistent. The `Activity` component in `theme.ts` is still using `React.createElement`, which should be fine. I'm now ready to make further changes.


**Refining Cleanup Function**

I'm now refining the `useEffect` cleanup function. The previous approach did correctly return `void`, by virtue of calling a function returned by `.subscribe()`, however the subscribe function returned a function which in turn returned a boolean. The correct fix is to ensure the cleanup function simply calls the returned function to satisfy React's requirement of returning `void`. I'm verifying this in `components/Sidebar.tsx` and `components/DownloadManager.tsx`.


**Verifying Icon Rendering**

I'm now focused on the rendering of icons from `lucide-react` within `theme.ts`. My goal is to ensure they render correctly. I've switched to `React.createElement` but need to ensure each icon renders as expected. I will confirm that the icons for `ExamSystem` components are displaying correctly.

I'm now validating the icon rendering. I am carefully examining each icon from `lucide-react` within `theme.ts` to confirm they display properly. I've switched to `React.createElement` to ensure compatibility. The focus is on the `ExamSystem` components. I'm verifying that the correct icons are shown for each component and that their styling is correct.


**Confirming Theme Colorization**

I'm now verifying the theme colorization for each `ExamSystem`. The properties include `glow`, `border`, `bg`, `activeBg`, `text`, `shadow`, `cardBg`, `cardBorder`, `cardAccent`, `secondary`, and `accent`, and I'm ensuring they're correctly applied and visually distinct. I'm focusing specifically on the visual effects these colors will produce.


**Verifying Musculoskeletal System**

I'm now verifying the Musculoskeletal system's properties in the theme. The icon renders correctly with `React.createElement`. The colors: glow, border, bg, activeBg, text, shadow, cardBg, cardBorder, cardAccent, secondary, and accent all appear appropriately applied in the visual design.


**Clarifying LucideIcon Usage**

I'm now clarifying the use of `LucideIcon` within `theme.ts`. It's used to type the component, not as a value. I've encountered the error "Activity' refers to a value, but is being used as a type." I will refactor to use `typeof Activity` and also address the "Cannot find name 'className'" error.

I'm wrapping up the implementation of tooltips in the download manager and drag-and-drop task functionality. I've ensured clear and responsive information for task purging and download actions. I'm making sure that states and actions are well-synced in the UI.


**Adding Tooltips to Actions**

I'm now adding tooltips to the actions within examination cards, focusing on icons. I'm leveraging the existing `Tooltip` component to give more context for various actions, making sure the user experience is smooth.


**Refining Download States**

I'm now integrating tooltips into download states in the `ExaminationCard`, clarifying progress. I'm focusing on "Stored," "Syncing," and "Offline" states. The goal is to provide visual feedback and clear context, which leverages the existing state management. I am also making sure that these tooltip states are visually easy for the user to understand.


**Finalizing Task Integration**

I'm focusing on providing useful and intuitive visual feedback that leverages the existing state management for user clarity.


**Refining Sync Logic**

I am now focusing on refining the sync logic within the `ExaminationCard`, and ensuring that the CloudDownload icons correctly display tooltips. The existing states are "Syncing", "Offline", and "Downloaded", and I'm updating the `ExaminationCard.tsx` component to handle this. I am carefully addressing hover interactions and making sure the information is easy to find.


**Integrating System Expansion**

I'm now integrating tooltips to system expansion headers within the application. My goal is to add clear context to the system section within the UI. I am leveraging the existing `Tooltip` component to improve the UI for the users.


**Adding System Context Tooltips**

I'm now integrating tooltips within system expansion headers, providing contextual information. This enhances the user experience, particularly within the UI's system sections. I'm leveraging the existing component and making sure it integrates smoothly.


**Refining Expansion Interactions**

I'm now refining the integration of tooltips within the system expansion headers and am focusing on the user experience. The headers' tooltips provide context within the UI and utilize the `Tooltip` component. I am refining hover interactions to make these tooltips appear more intuitively.


**Adding Sidebar Tooltips**

I'm now adding tooltips to the sidebar icons, focusing on enhancing user understanding of sidebar functions. These will be added to system icons, and I'm ensuring consistency and intuitive interactions within the existing component structure.


**Refining Icon Enhancements**

**Finalizing Application Tooltips**

I've now integrated tooltips throughout the application. I have ensured that hover interactions are consistent. I am now focused on polishing the existing implementations.


**Finalizing Icon Labels**

I've integrated and refined tooltips, focusing on accurate labels and intuitive interactions across the application. I have carefully optimized the visual consistency and made sure all states and animations match with the high-density aesthetic.


**Polishing Visual Consistency**

I am now focusing on visually polishing the tooltip implementation, ensuring consistent hover interactions and responsiveness throughout the application, and ensuring the high-density aesthetic is in order. I'm focusing on consistent animations and visual consistency across the board.
Apply subtle animations and transitions to the examination cards and content to make the interface more dynamic and engaging.

**Designing Tooltips for Icons**

I'm aiming to create a decoupled and modular `Tooltip` component to improve user interaction. This component will elegantly appear on hover, providing clear explanations for icons like the `Zap` and `Play` icons. The component will be versatile enough for wider application.


**Expanding Component Interaction**

I'm now expanding the scope of the `Tooltip` component beyond initial icons like `Zap` and `Play`. I'm integrating it with a wider range of interactive elements, including `ChevronDown`, `ChevronRight`, `Search`, and others. The component's design involves wrapping a child and displaying a consistent, glassmorphic-styled label on hover. I'm focusing on file updates, specifically updating the `Sidebar`, `ExamView`, `ExaminationCard`, `DownloadManager`, and `SystemSection` files. I'm aiming to apply subtle animations for a more dynamic interface, starting with the examination cards and content. I'll begin by creating the `Tooltip.tsx` file and updating the `ExaminationCard.tsx` file with this component and animations, then the `SystemSection.tsx`.


**Integrating Tooltips Across Modules**

I'm now integrating tooltips within more of the application's core modules. I've updated the `Sidebar`, and I'm currently working on `ExamView`, adding animations to further enhance the user experience. I'll include these updates into the `DownloadManager` component, as well. I'm focusing on creating a simple wrapper in React, not a strict function, to allow for more flexible implementation of tooltips throughout various components.

entries.description: Created a reusable, lightweight Tooltip component to provide context for interactive elements while maintaining the app's high-density aesthetic.
Added tooltips to sync buttons and enhanced hover animations for a more dynamic card experience.
Integrated tooltips for system status icons and added smooth transitions to the expansion trigger.
Added tooltips to all interactive icons in the protocol view and applied subtle entry animations to tabs and steps.
Added tooltips to sidebar navigation and search elements.
Integrated tooltips for queue controls and status icons in the Download Manager.

payload.text:
This is the table of contents from the 10th edition of talley and o'connor. You can see all of the things that they felt were important topics and subtopics in terms of clinical Examinations

Table of contents
Section 1: The general principles of history taking and physical examination

​​​​​​1. The general principles of history taking

2. Taking the history: advanced history taking

3. The general principles of physical examination
Section 2: The cardiovascular system

4. The cardiovascular history

5. The cardiac examination

6. The limb examination and peripheral vascular disease

7. Correlation of physical signs disease and cardiovascular disease

8. A summary of the cardiovascular examination and extending the cardiovascular examination

Section 3: The respiratory system

9. The respiratory history

10. The respiratory examination

11. Correlation of physical signs and respiratory disease

12. A summary of the respiratory examination and extending the respiratory examination

Section 4: The gastrointestinal system

13. The gastrointestinal history

14. The gastrointestinal examination

15. Correlation of physical signs and gastrointestinal disease

16. A summary of the gastrointestinal examination and extending the gastrointestinal examination

Section 5: The genitourinary system

17. The genitourinary history

18. The genitourinary examination

19. A summary of the examination of chronic kidney disease and extending the genitourinary examination

Section 6: The haematological system

20. The haematological history

21. The haematological examination

22. A summary of the haematological examination and extending the haematological examination

Section 7: The rheumatological system

23. The rheumatological history

24. The rheumatological examination

25. Correlation of physical signs with rheumatological and musculoskeletal disease

26. A summary of the rheumatological examination and extending the rheumatological examination

Section 8: The endocrine system

27. The endocrine history

28. The endocrine examination

29. Correlation of physical signs and endocrine disease

30. A summary of the endocrine examination and extending the endocrine examination

Section 9 The nervous system

31. The neurological history

32. The neurological examination: general signs and the cranial nerves

33. The neurological examination: speech and higher centres

34. The neurological examination: the peripheral nervous system

35. Correlation of physical signs and neurological syndromes and disease

36. A summary of the neurological examination and extending the neurological examinatio

Section 10: Paediatric and neonatal history and examination

37. The paediatric history and examination

38. The neonatal history and examination

Section 11: Women's health history and examination

39. The obstetric history and examination

40. The gynaecological history and examination

41. The breasts: history and examination

Section 12: Specialty system history and examination

42. The eyes, ears, nose and throat

43. The skin and lumps

44. The older person assessment

45. Approaching infectious diseases

Section 13: Mental health history and examination

46. The mental health history and mental state examination

Section 14: Acute care and end of life

47. The acutely ill patient

48. The pre-anaesthetic medical evaluation (PAME)

49. Assessment of death

Section 15: Adult history taking and examination in the wards and clinics

50. Writing and presenting the adult history and physical examination

51. A suggested method for a rapid screening adult physical examination

**Structuring the Exam Library**

I'm now focused on expanding the examination library, specifically the `EXAMINATIONS` constant, to mirror the Talley & O'Connor 10th edition table of contents. My plan is to structure the examinations by "Section" or "System", to better align with the textbook's organization. This structuring is the current priority.


**Refining System Categorization**

I've updated the `ExamSystem` enum in `types.ts` to reflect the newly expanded taxonomy of examinations, mirroring the structure found in Talley & O'Connor. My focus now is on integrating this categorization into the UI. I'll be updating `Sidebar.tsx` to handle the more granular system breakdown. I also anticipate needing to modify `useExaminations.ts` to accommodate the increased number of examination systems, all while maintaining the project's core principles of modularity and a clean aesthetic.


**Expanding the Categorization**

I'm now integrating the additional categories like Genitourinary, and Hematological into the `constants.ts` file, and adding placeholders, inspired by the textbook's structure. I'm also updating the `theme.ts` file to include color schemes and icons for the new systems. The `components/Sidebar.tsx` will be adapted to handle the expanded list of systems, possibly grouped by "Section". I'm making sure to mirror Talley & O'Connor terminology, so "Gastrointestinal" and "Rheumatological" it is!

entries.description: Updated ExamSystem enum to align with Talley & O'Connor 10th Edition taxonomy.
Expanded the examination library with sub-topics and categories based on Talley & O'Connor 10th Edition TOC.

entries.description: Updated sidebar icons and system handling to support the expanded Talley & O'Connor taxonomy.

payload.text:
The file i've attached is a very simplified summary of the various examinations and the things that should be examined.

Although the tables look to dry and clinical for my liking, The way that the examinations are presented and structured is simple and complete. It would be great if our exams could be structured in the same way, but with a newer, brighter and more colourful, interactive look.

**Aligning Content Structure**

I'm focused on integrating the ACRRM PDF's structure into the medical examination library app. I'm exploring methods to map the PDF's sections and subsections to the existing modular architecture, ensuring a smooth transition without breaking the decoupled design principles. The goal is to ensure all examinations are properly aligned to the reference.


**Enhancing User Interface**

I'm now integrating aesthetic improvements, specifically aligning the app's visual style with the user's desire for a more modern and engaging experience. I'm focusing on the protocol view, aiming to enhance the "interactive look" through a glassmorphism/high-density design, while mirroring the PDF's structured list. I'm also ensuring full alignment with the pre-existing colorful theme, and ensuring DRY principles are followed.


**Refining Component Interactions**

I'm now detailing component-specific updates for modular integration of the exam PDF. I'm focusing on updating the `types.ts` and `constants.ts` to reflect the specific hierarchical categorization in the PDF. I plan to enhance `ExamView.tsx` to handle sections, mirroring the PDF's structure within the protocol. Finally, I may tweak the theme.


**Categorizing Exam Steps**

I'm now implementing the new examination categories detailed earlier. I will update `constants.ts` to include these specific structural categorizations. Subsequently, I'm refactoring `ExamView.tsx` to group examination steps visually. I will expand the existing `category` field within the `ExamStep` type in `types.ts` to accommodate sub-headers within each section. The goal is a high-end, interactive checklist presentation.

To integrate the high-fidelity structure from the ACRRM Physical Exam Reference while elevating the "clinical dry" aesthetic into a vibrant, interactive dashboard, I have implemented the following updates:

1.  **Categorical Protocol Engine**: Updated `ExamView.tsx` to group steps into the specific headers found in the reference (e.g., "The Hands", "The Face", "Praecordium"). This creates a high-density, "scannable" interface that mirrors the PDF's logic but uses glassmorphic cards and system-specific accents.
2.  **Content Synthesis**: Expanded `constants.ts` with the exact taxonomies for Respiratory and Cardiovascular systems from the PDF, including specific clinical signs like "Splinter Hemorrhages" and "Schamroth's Window".
3.  **Interactive OSCE Mode**: Enhanced the "Checklist" functionality to provide real-time haptic-like visual feedback as you progress through the systemic categories.
4.  **Vibrant UI Overhaul**: Injected more saturated gradients and "glow" effects into `theme.ts` and `ExaminationCard.tsx` to move away from the paper-reference look into a "Tier 1 Medical App" aesthetic.

entries.description: Added category grouping support to ExamStep to allow for the structured "Sectional" layout found in medical reference textbooks.
Populated Respiratory and Cardiovascular examinations with the specific step-by-step structure from the ACRRM Physical Exam Reference.
